meta.title: SF-SPSNA-1: Native App Framework, Streamlit and Marketplace

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the Snowflake Native App Framework?

options.text: To package and distribute applications that run inside consumer Snowflake accounts
options.explanation: The Native App Framework is specifically designed for building, packaging, and distributing applications that consumers install in their own Snowflake accounts. Providers publish the app; consumers run it inside their own account boundary.

options.text: To replace all virtual warehouses with serverless compute
options.explanation: The Native App Framework has nothing to do with replacing virtual warehouses.

options.text: To enforce account-level password rotation policies
options.explanation: Password rotation is a security feature unrelated to the Native App Framework.

options.text: To continuously load CSV files from external stages
options.explanation: Continuous data loading is the purpose of Snowpipe, not the Native App Framework.

questions.stem: Which provider-side object holds versioned artifacts and metadata for a native app?

options.text: Reader account
options.explanation: Reader accounts are for sharing data with consumers who do not have Snowflake accounts, not for storing native app artifacts.

options.text: Masking policy
options.explanation: Masking policies are used for column-level data security, not artifact storage.

options.text: Application package
options.explanation: The application package is the provider-side container that stores versioned code artifacts, stages, and metadata before the app is released to consumers.

options.text: Warehouse cluster
options.explanation: Warehouse clusters are compute resources, not artifact repositories.

questions.stem: Where does an installed Snowflake native app run after a consumer installs it?

options.text: Only in the provider account on shared infrastructure
options.explanation: Native apps do not run in the provider account after installation; this would break consumer data isolation.

options.text: In the consumer Snowflake account boundary
options.explanation: Installed native apps run entirely within the consumer's Snowflake account boundary, which is central to Snowflake's isolation model and data governance.

options.text: In a Marketplace shared compute plane managed by Snowflake
options.explanation: There is no shared Marketplace compute plane; each consumer account provides its own compute.

options.text: On the end-user laptop via a local agent
options.explanation: Native apps run server-side in Snowflake, not on client laptops.

questions.stem: Why are references important in the Snowflake Native App Framework?

options.text: They replace application roles entirely within the installed app
options.explanation: References and application roles are separate concepts; references do not replace roles.

options.text: They automatically publish the app listing to the Marketplace
options.explanation: Marketplace listing is a separate workflow unrelated to references.

options.text: They let consumers grant the app controlled access to consumer-side objects
options.explanation: References are the approved mechanism by which a native app requests and consumers grant access to consumer-owned objects such as tables, views, or warehouses, without the provider ever seeing the underlying data directly.

options.text: They eliminate the need for a setup.sql script
options.explanation: Setup.sql is still required; references do not eliminate it.

questions.stem: What is the role of setup.sql in a Snowflake native app?

options.text: It defines MFA enrollment rules for the consumer account
options.explanation: MFA enrollment is an account security setting not controlled by setup.sql.

options.text: It sets warehouse defaults for the entire consumer account
options.explanation: Setup.sql does not set global account-level warehouse defaults.

options.text: It uploads code artifacts to the application package stage
options.explanation: Artifact uploads happen to the provider's stage before the package is released, not during install via setup.sql.

options.text: It runs the SQL needed to create app objects during installation or upgrade
options.explanation: Setup.sql is the installation and upgrade script that Snowflake automatically executes to create the required schemas, tables, procedures, and other objects when a consumer installs or upgrades the app.

questions.stem: Why is it significant that a native app runs inside the consumer account rather than the provider account?

options.text: The provider automatically becomes ACCOUNTADMIN in the consumer account
options.explanation: The provider does not gain ACCOUNTADMIN or any elevated privileges in the consumer account through app installation.

options.text: Consumer data can stay within the consumer's account boundary without being shared with the provider
options.explanation: Running the app inside the consumer account means consumer data never has to leave the consumer's Snowflake account boundary, supporting data isolation, governance, and compliance.

options.text: The app no longer requires any permissions from the consumer
options.explanation: The app still requires explicit permissions granted by the consumer.

options.text: Warehouses used by the app are billed to the provider
options.explanation: Compute costs are billed to the consumer's account, not the provider.

questions.stem: Which technology is most commonly embedded as the interactive UI layer for a Snowflake native app?

options.text: Snowpipe Streaming
options.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming is a data ingestion feature, not a UI technology.

options.text: Tri-Secret Secure encryption
options.explanation: Tri-Secret Secure is a data encryption option, not a UI framework.

options.text: Streamlit
options.explanation: Streamlit in Snowflake is the primary and most commonly used technology for building interactive web UIs inside native apps, allowing Python-based dashboards and data applications.

options.text: ODBC DSN configuration files
options.explanation: ODBC DSN files are connector configuration files for SQL clients, not UI frameworks.

questions.stem: Why should a developer use `st.cache_data` carefully in a Streamlit app embedded in a native app?

options.text: It grants database-level privileges to users who trigger the cache
options.explanation: `st.cache_data` caches Python function return values and has no relationship to database privileges.

options.text: It improves responsiveness by caching results, but stale data may be served if the underlying table changes
options.explanation: `st.cache_data` speeds up the app by reusing previous query results, but developers must set appropriate TTLs or manual cache invalidation to avoid serving stale data when the underlying Snowflake tables are updated.

options.text: It publishes the app to a paid listing automatically
options.explanation: Caching has no relationship to Marketplace listings.

options.text: It defines application roles for consumers
options.explanation: Application roles are defined in setup.sql and manifest.yml, not through Streamlit caching.

questions.stem: What operational overhead does Streamlit in Snowflake eliminate compared with self-hosting a separate web application tier?

options.text: Reduces micro-partition count for all tables in the account
options.explanation: Streamlit has no impact on micro-partition count.

options.text: Eliminates the need for any SQL queries in the app
options.explanation: Streamlit apps typically use SQL to query Snowflake data.

options.text: Eliminates the need to manage external servers, containers, or hosting infrastructure for the UI
options.explanation: Hosting Streamlit inside Snowflake means developers do not need to provision, maintain, or secure external web servers, container orchestration, or load balancers for the application UI layer.

options.text: Removes the need for permissions to be defined in the app
options.explanation: Permissions are still required and must be properly designed; hosting in Snowflake does not eliminate the need for access control.

questions.stem: A Streamlit native app needs governed access to consumer data. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Upload all consumer data to the provider account first for processing
options.explanation: Uploading consumer data to the provider account defeats the consumer-account isolation model and may violate data governance requirements.

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all end users of the app
options.explanation: Granting ACCOUNTADMIN to end users is a severe security anti-pattern that violates least-privilege principles.

options.text: Use granted references so consumers approve object access, and expose features through application roles
options.explanation: References let consumers explicitly approve what objects the app can access, and application roles ensure end users only access the features appropriate to their role, maintaining least-privilege access.

options.text: Disable consumer masking policies before running queries
options.explanation: Disabling masking policies would circumvent consumer data governance controls.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake capability is most directly used when a Streamlit app needs Python-based tabular data processing alongside SQL queries?

options.text: Snowpark
options.explanation: Snowpark provides Python (and Java/Scala) DataFrame APIs and stored procedure capabilities that pair naturally with Streamlit apps, enabling complex data transformations and ML inference to run server-side within Snowflake.

options.text: Network policies
options.explanation: Network policies restrict IP-based connectivity to Snowflake accounts and are not related to tabular data processing.

options.text: Reader accounts
options.explanation: Reader accounts are for sharing data with consumers who lack Snowflake accounts, not for data processing in apps.

options.text: Search optimization service
options.explanation: The search optimization service improves point-lookup query performance but is not a Python data processing capability.

questions.stem: Which file in a native app project declares application metadata, artifact locations, and the path to the setup script?

options.text: manifest.yml
options.explanation: Manifest.yml is the required configuration file for a native app. It declares the app's metadata, the location of setup.sql, default privileges, and references the app will request.

options.text: resource_monitor.sql
options.explanation: Resource_monitor.sql is not a native app project file; resource monitors are account-level objects created via SQL.

options.text: warehouse.conf
options.explanation: Warehouse.conf is not a Snowflake native app file format.

options.text: network_policy.ini
options.explanation: Network_policy.ini is not a Snowflake file format; network policies are created via SQL DDL.

questions.stem: Where are code artifacts for a native app commonly stored by the provider before the app is released to consumers?

options.text: Only on the developer's local filesystem
options.explanation: Local files must be uploaded to Snowflake before the app can be released.

options.text: Inside the account's query history
options.explanation: Query history stores executed SQL, not application code artifacts.

options.text: In stages associated with the application package
options.explanation: Code artifacts (Python files, setup.sql, manifest.yml, Streamlit files) are uploaded to Snowflake internal stages that are associated with the application package, where they are versioned and managed.

options.text: In a reader account shared with the consumer
options.explanation: Reader accounts are for data sharing with non-Snowflake users, not for storing native app artifacts.

questions.stem: What should an upgrade script in a native app primarily focus on?

options.text: Suspending all warehouses before running any upgrade logic
options.explanation: Suspending warehouses before upgrades is not a native app practice and would disrupt consumers.

options.text: Safely migrating app state and schema changes between versions
options.explanation: Upgrade scripts must handle schema migrations (ALTER TABLE, new objects, data backfills) in a way that preserves existing consumer data and configuration, ensuring a smooth transition from old to new versions.

options.text: Rotating all consumer user passwords
options.explanation: Native apps have no authority or need to rotate consumer user passwords.

options.text: Copying all consumer data to the provider account
options.explanation: Copying consumer data to the provider is a fundamental violation of the native app trust model.

questions.stem: Why is idempotent setup logic important in native app installation and upgrade scripts?

options.text: It increases warehouse concurrency limits automatically
options.explanation: Idempotent logic has no effect on warehouse concurrency settings.

options.text: It makes installs and upgrades safer when scripts are retried after partial failures
options.explanation: Idempotent scripts (using `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`, `CREATE OR REPLACE`, etc.) produce the same end state regardless of how many times they run. This is critical when installs or upgrades fail partway through and must be retried.

options.text: It removes the need for consumer-granted references
options.explanation: References are still required for consumer object access.

options.text: It guarantees paid subscription renewal
options.explanation: Paid subscriptions are a Marketplace commercial concern unrelated to script idempotency.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of an application role in a native app?

options.text: Replacing all account-level roles in the consumer account
options.explanation: Application roles are scoped to the installed app, not the entire consumer account.

options.text: Controlling virtual warehouse sizing for the app
options.explanation: Application roles are about access control, not compute resource sizing.

options.text: Storing Streamlit cache data on behalf of users
options.explanation: Application roles are access control objects, not data storage mechanisms.

options.text: Exposing app-specific privileges that consumers can grant to their users
options.explanation: Application roles define the functional access tiers within the app (e.g., ADMIN, VIEWER) that consumers can grant to their users, enabling least-privilege access to app features.

questions.stem: Where can providers list native apps so that Snowflake customers can discover and install them?

options.text: Snowflake Marketplace
options.explanation: Snowflake Marketplace is the official distribution channel where providers list their native apps, data products, and services, making them discoverable and installable by eligible Snowflake customers.

options.text: The result cache browser in Snowsight
options.explanation: The result cache browser is a Snowsight interface for viewing cached query results, not a distribution channel.

options.text: The query profile viewer
options.explanation: The query profile viewer is a performance analysis tool for examining query execution plans.

options.text: The user stage of the provider account
options.explanation: User stages are private storage areas in individual Snowflake accounts, not a discovery platform.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of a trial listing in Snowflake Marketplace?

options.text: To permanently disable all paid features for the consumer
options.explanation: Trial listings provide a time-limited or feature-limited evaluation, not permanent disabling of paid features.

options.text: To bypass application package versioning requirements
options.explanation: Trials still go through the same packaging and versioning workflow as paid listings.

options.text: To let consumers evaluate an app before committing to a purchase or subscription
options.explanation: Trial listings give potential consumers a time- or feature-limited version of the app so they can evaluate its value before committing to a paid subscription or purchase.

options.text: To replace the setup.sql installation script
options.explanation: Setup.sql is a technical app installation artifact unrelated to the trial listing commercial model.

questions.stem: Which monetization model charges consumers based on how much they actually use the native app?

options.text: Mandatory ACCOUNTADMIN pricing tier
options.explanation: There is no Snowflake pricing model based on ACCOUNTADMIN role usage.

options.text: Micro-partition count pricing
options.explanation: Micro-partition count is an internal Snowflake storage concept, not a Marketplace pricing model.

options.text: Role hierarchy flat pricing
options.explanation: Role hierarchy is an access control concept, not a commercial pricing model.

options.text: Usage-based pricing
options.explanation: Usage-based pricing aligns fees to measured app consumption metrics such as the number of queries processed, rows analyzed, or API calls made through the app.

questions.stem: Why should providers review Marketplace listing requirements before submitting their native app for publication?

options.text: The review process automatically creates warehouses in the provider account
options.explanation: The Marketplace review process does not create warehouses; it evaluates the app against listing criteria.

options.text: The review replaces application roles with Snowflake-managed roles
options.explanation: The review does not modify application roles within the app.

options.text: Listings must satisfy packaging standards, security expectations, and commercial documentation to be approved
options.explanation: Snowflake's Marketplace review process evaluates submissions against packaging requirements, security expectations (least-privilege permissions), and commercial completeness (descriptions, pricing, terms).

options.text: Only free apps undergo review; paid apps are automatically published
options.explanation: All Marketplace listings, both free and paid, go through review.

questions.stem: What does enabling auto-fulfillment for a Marketplace listing mean for eligible consumers?

options.text: The app package no longer requires a version before publishing
options.explanation: Versioning is still required; auto-fulfillment does not bypass packaging requirements.

options.text: All consumer data is automatically shared with the provider
options.explanation: Auto-fulfillment relates to install approval workflow, not data sharing.

options.text: Eligible consumers can install the app without needing manual provider approval for each request
options.explanation: Auto-fulfillment streamlines the consumer installation experience by automatically approving eligible consumers without requiring the provider to manually review each individual install request.

options.text: No application roles are required within the app
options.explanation: Application roles are still required for proper access control regardless of fulfillment mode.

questions.stem: Why are application roles a key security feature in native apps?

options.text: They let consumers grant only the specific privileges needed for each app feature to their users
options.explanation: Application roles expose least-privilege access tiers within the app. A consumer can grant a user the VIEWER role (read-only features) or the ADMIN role (management features) rather than giving all users access to all app capabilities.

options.text: They bypass Snowflake's RBAC model for app users
options.explanation: Application roles work within Snowflake's RBAC model, not outside it.

options.text: They force the provider to own all consumer data accessed by the app
options.explanation: The native app model specifically prevents providers from owning or accessing consumer data directly.

options.text: They replace references as the consumer object access mechanism
options.explanation: Application roles and references serve different purposes: references control object access, while roles control feature access.

questions.stem: A native app needs to make outbound calls to an external REST API. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Open arbitrary unrestricted internet access from within the app by default
options.explanation: Native apps do not have arbitrary internet access by default; outbound access must be explicitly requested and approved.

options.text: Request the EXTERNAL_ACCESS_INTEGRATION privilege and define allowed endpoints using an external access integration
options.explanation: External access from native apps requires defining an EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION that specifies the allowed network rules (specific hostnames and ports), and the app must request permission to use it.

options.text: Embed API keys in the listing description text
options.explanation: Embedding credentials in listing descriptions is a severe security anti-pattern.

options.text: Disable consumer governance policies to allow outbound network traffic
options.explanation: Disabling consumer governance policies is not possible from within a native app.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes the native app trust model between providers and consumers?

options.text: Providers automatically receive all privileges in the consumer account upon installation
options.explanation: Providers receive no automatic privileges in the consumer account; all access is explicitly requested and consumer-approved.

options.text: Consumers cannot inspect which permissions an app is requesting
options.explanation: Consumers can review all requested permissions before granting them.

options.text: Providers should request only the minimum consumer permissions necessary for each app feature
options.explanation: The trust model is built on least privilege: providers should only request permissions strictly necessary for the features they offer, making it easier for consumers to trust and install the app.

options.text: Application roles are optional and can be omitted for simpler apps
options.explanation: Application roles are the fundamental mechanism for least-privilege feature access within the app.

questions.stem: What is the main risk of requesting excessively broad privileges in a native app?

options.text: Broader privileges improve Streamlit rendering performance
options.explanation: Privilege scope has no effect on UI rendering performance.

options.text: Broad privilege requests reduce consumer trust and create a larger blast radius if the app is compromised
options.explanation: Requesting more permissions than necessary makes consumers less likely to trust and install the app, and if the app or the provider were ever compromised, a wider permission set means more consumer data and objects are at risk.

options.text: Smaller app packages result from requesting broader permissions
options.explanation: Package size is determined by code artifacts, not permission scope.

options.text: Excessively broad privileges guarantee automatic Marketplace promotion
options.explanation: Marketplace promotion depends on listing quality and consumer ratings, not permission breadth.

questions.stem: Which statement most accurately reflects provider visibility into consumer data within the native app model?

options.text: Providers automatically inherit SYSADMIN in the consumer account
options.explanation: Providers inherit no account roles in the consumer account through app installation.

options.text: Providers should assume they cannot access consumer data beyond what is explicitly granted through references
options.explanation: The native app model is designed so providers have no visibility into consumer data beyond what the consumer explicitly grants through references. Providers should design their apps with this boundary in mind.

options.text: Marketplace analytics expose every row of consumer data to the provider
options.explanation: Marketplace analytics provide providers with aggregate usage telemetry, not access to consumer data rows.

options.text: App installs automatically disable consumer masking policies
options.explanation: App installation does not affect masking policies; consumer governance controls remain fully in effect.

questions.stem: Why should a native app developer test in a separate consumer-style Snowflake environment rather than the provider development environment?

options.text: To make warehouses free in the development environment
options.explanation: Testing environments are not free; costs are incurred in any Snowflake account.

options.text: To bypass the application packaging process during testing
options.explanation: Packaging must still be completed before the app can be installed in the test environment.

options.text: To validate installation, permission grants, and upgrade behavior from the consumer's perspective
options.explanation: A consumer-style test environment forces the developer to experience the app the same way a consumer would: going through the full install flow, granting references, using application roles, and triggering upgrade scripts.

options.text: To disable setup.sql execution during testing
options.explanation: Setup.sql should still execute in the test environment; skipping it would invalidate the test.

questions.stem: If setup.sql fails during native app installation, what should the developer inspect first?

options.text: The Marketplace pricing page for the listing
options.explanation: The Marketplace pricing page is irrelevant to setup.sql execution failures.

options.text: Error messages and the SQL object dependency ordering in setup.sql
options.explanation: Installation failures in setup.sql most commonly stem from SQL errors or incorrect dependency ordering. The error output in the installation log identifies the failing statement.

options.text: Warehouse logo configuration
options.explanation: Warehouse logos are a display preference unrelated to setup.sql errors.

options.text: The result cache TTL settings
options.explanation: Result cache TTL is a query performance setting unrelated to setup.sql failures.

questions.stem: Why is automated testing of Snowpark logic inside a native app particularly valuable before packaging a release?

options.text: Automated tests automatically increase the app's Marketplace ranking
options.explanation: Marketplace rankings are based on consumer ratings and listing quality, not test coverage.

options.text: Automated tests remove the need for application role design
options.explanation: Automated tests have no effect on the need for application role design.

options.text: Automated tests catch logic regressions before the code is packaged and released to consumers
options.explanation: Automated tests that run as part of the CI/CD pipeline catch regressions in Snowpark logic before a new version is packaged and released, preventing broken functionality from reaching consumers.

options.text: Automated tests guarantee backward compatibility across all version upgrades
options.explanation: Automated tests do not guarantee backward compatibility; they must be designed to test specific upgrade scenarios.

questions.stem: Which tooling is most commonly used during iterative native app development for packaging and testing workflows?

options.text: Snowflake Fail-Safe recovery
options.explanation: Fail-Safe is a Snowflake storage recovery feature for tables, not a development tool.

options.text: Reader accounts only
options.explanation: Reader accounts are for data sharing with non-Snowflake users, not for app development workflows.

options.text: Micro-partition clustering keys
options.explanation: Clustering keys are a table optimization feature unrelated to native app development.

options.text: Snowflake CLI (snow)
options.explanation: The Snowflake CLI (snow) provides native commands for native app development: `snow app run` deploys the app from source to a dev environment, `snow app teardown` removes it, and the CLI handles artifact upload and version management automatically.

questions.stem: Why is testing upgrade scripts with realistic consumer state data more valuable than testing with empty databases?

options.text: It guarantees that the public listing will be approved by Snowflake
options.explanation: Listing approval depends on packaging and security review, not test data realism.

options.text: It reveals schema migration issues and conflicts that only appear when upgrading from a previous version with existing data
options.explanation: Upgrade scripts must handle pre-existing tables, data, and configurations that a previous version created. Testing against realistic consumer state reveals migration failures and data transformation issues that a clean install test would never encounter.

options.text: It removes version numbers from the app package
options.explanation: Version numbers are part of the application package metadata and are not affected by test data.

options.text: It disables role grants during upgrade execution
options.explanation: Role grants are defined in setup.sql and the manifest, not governed by test data volume.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly describe the Snowflake Native App Framework? (Select TWO)

options.text: It allows providers to build and distribute apps that run inside consumer Snowflake accounts
options.explanation: The Native App Framework's core purpose is enabling providers to package and distribute apps that run inside consumer Snowflake accounts.

options.text: It uses an application package as the provider-side container for versioned artifacts
options.explanation: The application package is the foundational provider-side object that holds versioned code, stages, and metadata for the app before release.

options.text: It replaces virtual warehouses with serverless compute for all app workloads
options.explanation: The Native App Framework does not replace virtual warehouses; apps still use consumer-provisioned warehouses for compute.

options.text: It requires consumer data to be copied to the provider account before processing
options.explanation: Consumer data never needs to move to the provider; the app runs in the consumer account and accesses consumer data locally.

questions.stem: Which TWO mechanisms allow a native app to access consumer-owned objects safely? (Select TWO)

options.text: References that consumers explicitly grant to the app
options.explanation: References are the approved mechanism for a native app to request access to specific consumer-owned objects, with the consumer approving each reference.

options.text: Application roles that consumers assign to their users for app features
options.explanation: Application roles define which features and objects within the app a consumer user can access, enabling least-privilege access control.

options.text: Masking policies automatically disabled by the app at install time
options.explanation: Native apps cannot disable consumer masking policies; consumer governance controls are preserved.

options.text: The provider inheriting SYSADMIN in the consumer account
options.explanation: Providers receive no account roles in the consumer account through app installation.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements are true about where a native app runs after a consumer installs it? (Select TWO)

options.text: The app's objects and logic reside in the consumer's Snowflake account
options.explanation: After installation, the app's schemas, procedures, and other objects are created inside the consumer's Snowflake account.

options.text: Consumer data processed by the app stays within the consumer account boundary
options.explanation: Since the app runs in the consumer account, any consumer data it processes never has to leave the consumer's account boundary.

options.text: The app is executed on the provider's Snowflake servers
options.explanation: The app runs in the consumer account, not on the provider's servers.

options.text: The app runs on a shared compute plane managed by Snowflake Marketplace
options.explanation: There is no shared Marketplace compute plane; each consumer account provides its own compute resources.

questions.stem: Which TWO project files are required components of a Snowflake native app? (Select TWO)

options.text: manifest.yml — declares app metadata and the setup script location
options.explanation: Manifest.yml is a required file that Snowflake reads to understand the app's structure, including the setup script path, version, and requested privileges.

options.text: setup.sql — executed during installation and upgrade to create app objects
options.explanation: Setup.sql is the required installation and upgrade script that Snowflake executes when consumers install or upgrade the app.

options.text: warehouse.conf — defines compute resources for the app
options.explanation: Warehouse.conf is not a Snowflake native app file format; warehouses are created via SQL DDL.

options.text: network_policy.ini — restricts IP access to the app
options.explanation: Network_policy.ini is not a Snowflake file format; network policies are created via SQL.

questions.stem: Which TWO practices make native app setup scripts more reliable? (Select TWO)

options.text: Writing idempotent SQL using CREATE IF NOT EXISTS or CREATE OR REPLACE patterns
options.explanation: Idempotent patterns (IF NOT EXISTS, CREATE OR REPLACE) allow setup scripts to be safely retried after partial failures.

options.text: Ordering object creation so dependencies are created before the objects that depend on them
options.explanation: SQL object dependencies must be respected: create parent tables before views that reference them, create stages before procedures that reference them.

options.text: Granting ACCOUNTADMIN to the app's service account in setup.sql
options.explanation: Granting ACCOUNTADMIN to any service account is a severe security anti-pattern.

options.text: Copying all consumer tables into the app's schema during setup
options.explanation: Copying consumer tables would violate the data isolation model.

questions.stem: Which TWO are benefits of hosting a Streamlit app inside Snowflake rather than on an external web server? (Select TWO)

options.text: No external servers or containers are required for the UI tier
options.explanation: Snowflake hosts and manages the Streamlit application server, removing the need for external infrastructure such as EC2 instances, containers, or load balancers.

options.text: Streamlit apps can query Snowflake data within the same security boundary without data egress
options.explanation: A Streamlit app running inside Snowflake queries data within the same account boundary, so no data needs to be transferred outside Snowflake, reducing egress costs and latency.

options.text: Streamlit in Snowflake eliminates the need for any SQL queries
options.explanation: Streamlit apps typically use SQL heavily to query and display Snowflake data.

options.text: All Snowflake accounts get the Streamlit app pre-installed automatically
options.explanation: Streamlit apps must be explicitly created and shared; they are not automatically installed in consumer accounts.

questions.stem: Which TWO Snowflake capabilities are commonly combined with Streamlit when building a native app that performs Python data processing? (Select TWO)

options.text: Snowpark — for Python DataFrame operations and stored procedures
options.explanation: Snowpark provides the Python DataFrame API and stored procedure support that Streamlit apps use to perform complex transformations server-side within Snowflake.

options.text: Snowflake Cortex — for AI/ML inference functions callable from Python
options.explanation: Snowflake Cortex provides built-in AI functions (sentiment analysis, summarization, embeddings) that can be called from Python or SQL within a Streamlit native app to add ML capabilities without external services.

options.text: Fail-Safe — for recovering accidentally deleted app data
options.explanation: Fail-Safe is a background data recovery feature for tables, not something developers integrate with Streamlit.

options.text: Tri-Secret Secure — for managing Streamlit session encryption keys
options.explanation: Tri-Secret Secure is a data encryption option for at-rest data, not a Streamlit session feature.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about application roles in native apps are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Application roles are defined in setup.sql using CREATE APPLICATION ROLE
options.explanation: Application roles are created with the CREATE APPLICATION ROLE SQL command within setup.sql, defining the access tiers available within the app.

options.text: Consumers can grant application roles to their users or account roles
options.explanation: Once the app is installed, consumers can grant application roles to their own users or to account roles, enabling controlled access to app features.

options.text: Application roles replace account roles globally across the consumer account
options.explanation: Application roles are scoped entirely to the installed application, not to the entire consumer account.

options.text: Application roles are created in the provider account and pushed to consumers
options.explanation: Application roles are defined in provider-authored setup.sql but are created inside the consumer account during installation.

questions.stem: Which TWO distribution options are available to providers when sharing a native app with consumers? (Select TWO)

options.text: Public Marketplace listing — discoverable by any eligible Snowflake customer
options.explanation: Providers can publish apps to the public Snowflake Marketplace where any eligible Snowflake customer can discover and install them.

options.text: Private listing — shared directly with specific consumer accounts
options.explanation: Providers can also create private listings targeted at specific Snowflake account identifiers, useful for enterprise or partner distribution.

options.text: Reader account distribution — pushes the app to reader accounts
options.explanation: Reader accounts are for non-Snowflake users accessing shared data; native apps require consumers to have their own Snowflake accounts.

options.text: Stage export — consumers download a ZIP file from an external URL
options.explanation: Native apps are not distributed as downloadable ZIP files.

questions.stem: Which TWO types of monetization models are available for native apps on the Snowflake Marketplace? (Select TWO)

options.text: Usage-based pricing — fees based on measured app consumption
options.explanation: Usage-based pricing aligns fees to measurable consumption signals such as queries processed, rows analyzed, or API calls made.

options.text: Subscription pricing — recurring flat fees for access to the app
options.explanation: Subscription pricing provides consumers with app access for a recurring fee (monthly, annual), regardless of usage volume.

options.text: Role hierarchy pricing — fees scale with the number of consumer account roles
options.explanation: Role hierarchy pricing is not a Snowflake Marketplace pricing model.

options.text: Micro-partition pricing — fees based on the number of consumer table partitions
options.explanation: Micro-partition count is an internal Snowflake storage metric, not a basis for Marketplace pricing.

questions.stem: Which TWO actions should a provider take to request external network access from a native app? (Select TWO)

options.text: Define an EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION specifying the allowed network rules (hostnames and ports)
options.explanation: An EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION defines exactly which external hosts and ports the app is allowed to contact, making outbound network access explicit and auditable.

options.text: Declare the required external access integration in the app's manifest.yml
options.explanation: The manifest.yml must declare the required external access integration so that consumers are informed of and can approve the external connectivity during installation.

options.text: Embed API credentials in the listing description for consumer reference
options.explanation: Embedding credentials in listing descriptions is a severe security anti-pattern.

options.text: Open unrestricted internet access by default in the application package
options.explanation: Unrestricted internet access is never available by default in native apps.

questions.stem: Which TWO are valid strategies for debugging a native app installation failure? (Select TWO)

options.text: Review the error output from the installation to identify the failing SQL statement in setup.sql
options.explanation: Snowflake's installation error output identifies which statement in setup.sql failed and why, providing the starting point for diagnosis.

options.text: Run setup.sql statements manually in a development environment to isolate the failing step
options.explanation: Running setup.sql statements individually in a development environment allows the developer to isolate the specific statement causing the failure and test fixes interactively.

options.text: Bypass setup.sql by using a pre-built image from the Marketplace debug console
options.explanation: There is no Marketplace debug console that bypasses setup.sql.

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to the app to resolve all permission-related install failures
options.explanation: Granting ACCOUNTADMIN is a severe security anti-pattern; permission issues should be resolved by properly defining privileges.

questions.stem: Which TWO Snowflake CLI commands support the native app development loop? (Select TWO)

options.text: snow app run — deploys the app from source to a development environment
options.explanation: `snow app run` automates the upload of source files and installation of the app in a developer account, enabling fast iteration.

options.text: snow app teardown — removes the installed app and application package from the development environment
options.explanation: `snow app teardown` removes the application object and package from the development environment, allowing a clean reinstall to test the full installation flow.

options.text: snow app publish — immediately makes the app available on the public Marketplace
options.explanation: `snow app publish` is not a standard Snowflake CLI command for direct Marketplace publishing.

options.text: snow app encrypt — encrypts all stage files for the app
options.explanation: `snow app encrypt` is not a Snowflake CLI command.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about versioning in native apps are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Providers create a version in the application package using ALTER APPLICATION PACKAGE ... ADD VERSION
options.explanation: Providers add versions to the application package using the ALTER APPLICATION PACKAGE ... ADD VERSION command, which snapshots the current staged artifacts into a versioned, immutable release.

options.text: A release directive controls which version consumers with specific accounts receive
options.explanation: Release directives allow providers to target specific versions to specific consumer accounts or account groups, enabling staged rollouts.

options.text: Consumers choose which patch version to install from a Marketplace menu
options.explanation: Consumers do not pick individual patch versions from a menu; version selection is controlled by the provider through release directives.

options.text: Each new version in the application package automatically replaces the existing version for all consumers
options.explanation: Adding a new version does not automatically push it to all consumers; the release directive must be updated.

questions.stem: Which TWO behaviors characterize the provider's access to consumer data within the native app model? (Select TWO)

options.text: Providers cannot directly query consumer tables unless the consumer explicitly grants a reference
options.explanation: Providers have no default access to consumer tables. Access is only possible when a consumer explicitly grants it through the reference binding process.

options.text: Consumer masking policies and row access policies remain in effect for data accessed through references
options.explanation: Even when a consumer grants a reference to a table, Snowflake's governance policies (masking, row access) continue to apply, protecting sensitive data.

options.text: Providers automatically inherit SELECT on all consumer tables after installation
options.explanation: Installation grants no data access to providers; each object access requires explicit consumer approval.

options.text: Provider app code can disable consumer governance controls during execution
options.explanation: Native apps cannot disable consumer governance controls; those controls are enforced by Snowflake at the platform level.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about the Marketplace listing review process are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Listings must include accurate descriptions, screenshots, and pricing information
options.explanation: Listing completeness — accurate descriptions, feature documentation, screenshots, and pricing details — is evaluated during the review process.

options.text: Security review evaluates whether the app requests only the permissions it needs
options.explanation: The security review checks that the app follows least-privilege principles and does not request excessive or unjustified permissions.

options.text: Only free apps undergo security review; paid apps are automatically approved
options.explanation: All listings, regardless of pricing model, are subject to review.

options.text: Review automatically creates a test warehouse in the provider account
options.explanation: The review process evaluates the submitted listing content; it does not create resources in the provider's account.

questions.stem: Which TWO object types can a native app create in the consumer account through setup.sql? (Select TWO)

options.text: Schemas and tables within the app's database
options.explanation: Setup.sql can create schemas and tables within the native app's installed database object in the consumer account.

options.text: Stored procedures and UDFs registered under the app
options.explanation: Setup.sql can define stored procedures and UDFs scoped to the app, providing the business logic and data processing capabilities.

options.text: New Snowflake user accounts for end users
options.explanation: Native apps cannot create Snowflake user accounts; user management is the consumer account admin's responsibility.

options.text: Account-level network policies affecting all connections
options.explanation: Native apps cannot create account-level network policies or other account-wide security configurations.

questions.stem: Which TWO considerations are important when designing a Streamlit app within a native app for multi-consumer use? (Select TWO)

options.text: The app must use references rather than hardcoded table names to access consumer data
options.explanation: Consumer data schemas and table names vary across installations. Using references (not hardcoded names) allows each consumer to bind the app to their specific objects.

options.text: Application roles should gate access to different Streamlit pages based on consumer user roles
options.explanation: Application roles can control which pages and features of the Streamlit app each consumer user can access, implementing least-privilege within the UI itself.

options.text: The Streamlit app must upload consumer data to the provider before displaying it
options.explanation: Uploading consumer data to the provider is a fundamental violation of the native app data isolation model.

options.text: All consumers should use the same hardcoded warehouse name defined by the provider
options.explanation: Hardcoding a warehouse name would fail for most consumers. The app should allow consumers to specify or bind a warehouse through a reference.

questions.stem: Which TWO approaches can be used to test Snowpark stored procedure logic in a native app? (Select TWO)

options.text: Unit test the Python handler functions locally using pytest before packaging
options.explanation: Python handler functions contain pure Python logic that can be extracted and unit-tested locally with pytest, providing fast feedback before any Snowflake deployment.

options.text: Install the app in a consumer-style test account and call the procedures via CALL statements
options.explanation: Integration testing in a consumer-style test account validates the full end-to-end flow including installation, privilege grants, procedure execution, and expected output.

options.text: Bypass Snowflake security controls in the test environment to simplify testing
options.explanation: Bypassing security controls in test environments can mask real bugs that will surface in production consumer accounts.

options.text: Use the Marketplace sandbox to run untested procedure code in production consumer accounts
options.explanation: Running untested code in production consumer accounts risks corrupting consumer data.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about secrets management in native apps are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Snowflake SECRET objects can store credentials that the app uses for external API calls
options.explanation: Snowflake SECRET objects securely store credentials (API keys, OAuth tokens) that can be accessed by stored procedures and UDFs during execution without exposing the values in code.

options.text: Secrets used by the app should be declared in the manifest so consumers can approve their use
options.explanation: Secrets that the app needs to use must be declared in the manifest so consumers are aware of and can approve the credential usage before installation.

options.text: API credentials should be stored as plain text in the app's setup.sql for easy access
options.explanation: Storing credentials in plain text in setup.sql means they are visible in the app's code artifact and pose a severe security risk.

options.text: Secrets can be written to the app's Streamlit session state so users can view them if needed
options.explanation: Credentials should never be exposed to end users through session state or UI elements.

questions.stem: Which TWO Marketplace features help providers track how consumers are using their native app? (Select TWO)

options.text: Marketplace analytics — provides aggregate install and usage event data to providers
options.explanation: Snowflake Marketplace analytics provides providers with aggregate telemetry about listing views, installs, and basic usage signals without exposing consumer data.

options.text: Usage tracking tables within the app — custom telemetry the provider writes to app-owned tables
options.explanation: Providers can design the app to log usage events to app-owned tables within the consumer account, which can be shared back to the provider through a data share, providing richer usage insights.

options.text: Direct SELECT access to all consumer databases granted automatically to providers
options.explanation: Providers are never granted direct database access in consumer accounts.

options.text: Snowflake audit logs from consumer accounts shared automatically with providers
options.explanation: Consumer audit logs are not automatically shared with providers; they remain under the consumer's control.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about the relationship between application packages and versions are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: An application package can contain multiple versions (e.g., v1.0, v1.1, v2.0) simultaneously
options.explanation: Providers maintain multiple versions in a single application package simultaneously, enabling staged rollouts where different consumers run different versions.

options.text: A version in an application package is immutable after it is added
options.explanation: Once a version is added to an application package, its artifact snapshot is immutable. To update code, providers must add a new version or patch.

options.text: Consumers automatically receive the latest version without provider action
options.explanation: Consumers receive new versions only when the provider updates the release directive targeting them.

options.text: A version cannot be associated with a specific stage path containing its artifacts
options.explanation: Each version is created from a specific stage path that contains the manifest.yml, setup.sql, and other artifacts.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about upgrade behavior in native apps are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Upgrade scripts should use ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN rather than DROP and recreate to avoid data loss
options.explanation: Dropping and recreating tables in an upgrade script destroys consumer data. Using ALTER TABLE to add or modify columns safely migrates schema while preserving existing data.

options.text: Testing upgrades from a previous version with consumer state is essential before releasing a new version
options.explanation: Upgrade scripts may fail when encountering pre-existing consumer data or configuration. Testing with realistic consumer state before release is essential.

options.text: Upgrade scripts automatically roll back if any step fails without developer intervention
options.explanation: Upgrade scripts do not automatically roll back on failure; developers must design their scripts with idempotency and manual recovery in mind.

options.text: Providers can skip version numbers when releasing without any impact on consumers
options.explanation: Skipping versions can cause upgrade path issues for consumers who are upgrading from an intermediate version.

questions.stem: Which TWO security practices are recommended when developing native apps? (Select TWO)

options.text: Request the minimum necessary permissions in the manifest to maintain consumer trust
options.explanation: Requesting only necessary permissions reduces consumer concern about installing the app and limits the potential damage if the app or provider were ever compromised.

options.text: Define fine-grained application roles rather than a single all-access role
options.explanation: Fine-grained application roles (e.g., separate VIEWER, ANALYST, and ADMIN roles) allow consumers to grant each user only what they need, implementing least-privilege at the feature level.

options.text: Request ACCOUNTADMIN-equivalent permissions to ensure all app features work without errors
options.explanation: Requesting ACCOUNTADMIN-level permissions is a security anti-pattern that will cause Marketplace review rejection and deter consumers.

options.text: Disable Snowflake's automatic audit logging within the app to reduce noise
options.explanation: Snowflake's audit logging cannot be disabled by a native app, and disabling audit logs would violate consumer security requirements.
